summary: Static testability-surface analyzer that surfaces welded boundary calls (clock, subprocess, network, file I/O, randomness) with no seam to inject a failure — a deterministic, zero-LLM testability backlog, not a bug list; intended as an advisory input to the `quality` skill.

notes: pry has no published release yet (Layer-0 / Stage-1). Build from source in a sibling `pry` checkout: `cargo build --release` (Rust >= 1.85).

Point quality at the local build with `PRY_BIN=/abs/path/to/pry/target/release/pry`; this maintainer-local override is the supported delivery until a tagged release installer (cargo-dist, the nose model) lands.
Use `PRY_BIN` only as a maintainer-local override when testing a sibling checkout; a future release will install a `pry` binary on PATH.
Rebuild from the sibling checkout (`git pull && cargo build --release`) to update a source-built `pry`.

detect.failure_hint: Build `pry` from source (`cargo build --release`) and put it on PATH or set `PRY_BIN`, before using the quality testability backlog.

healthcheck.failure_hint: The `pry` binary exists but its `map` surface is unavailable. Rebuild from a current source checkout.

version_expectation.constraint: Prefer a source build from a recent `pry` main; no tagged release yet.

when_missing: Quality keeps its deterministic gates authoritative and omits the welded-at-demand testability backlog.
Do not claim a testability or injectability review succeeded without a healthy `pry` binary.
when_version_mismatch: Treat changes in welded/seamed classification, demand-subset ranking, or JSON output shape as advisory drift before promoting findings into gates.

host_notes: `pry` is an advisory input to Charness `quality`: it proposes a testability backlog (welded boundary calls with no injection seam), not a bug list, and must never fail standing quality. This manifest makes `pry` discoverable and doctor-checkable; the `quality` invocation driver (the consuming skill) ships separately and is not yet wired.

pry analyzes TypeScript/JavaScript today — the validated surface (see pry `docs/precision-gate.md`). A Python frontend is out of scope: the author's Python is welded glue with no discrimination (pry `docs/kill-gate.md`).
Run `pry map <path>` for the full finding map, or `pry map <path> --summary-only` for the coverage summary; the `demand=true` substitution-demand subset is the ranked backlog.
